content:
# Genesis Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 10r-10v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_018_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_018_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 13-14 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0017

---

*The Three Men at Mamre — Abraham's Hospitality (vv. 1–8)*

**18:1** And the LORD appeared [וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה] to him [אֵלָיו] at the terebinths of Mamre [בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא] — and he was sitting [וְהוּא יֹשֵׁב] at the entrance of the tent [פֶּתַח הָאֹהֶל] at the heat of the day [כְּחֹם הַיּוֹם].

**18:2** And he lifted up his eyes [וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו] and he looked [וַיַּרְא] — and look [וְהִנֵּה] — three men [שְׁלֹשָׁה אֲנָשִׁים] were standing over against him [נִצָּבִים עָלָיו] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] and he ran [וַיָּרָץ] to meet them [לִקְרָאתָם] from the entrance of the tent [מִפֶּתַח הָאֹהֶל] and he bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] to the ground [אָרְצָה].

**18:3** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: My lord [אֲדֹנָי] — if now I have found favor [אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֶיךָ] — do not pass by [אַל-נָא תַעֲבֹר] from upon your servant [מֵעַל עַבְדֶּךָ].

**18:4** Let now a little water be taken [יֻקַּח-נָא מְעַט-מַיִם] and wash your feet [וְרַחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיכֶם] and recline [וְהִשָּׁעֲנוּ] under the tree [תַּחַת הָעֵץ].

**18:5** And I will take [וְאֶקְחָה] a morsel of bread [פַת-לֶחֶם] and sustain your heart [וְסַעֲדוּ לִבְּכֶם] — afterward [אַחַר] you shall pass on [תַּעֲבֹרוּ] — for on this account [כִּי-עַל-כֵּן] you have passed by your servant [עֲבַרְתֶּם עַל-עַבְדְּכֶם] — and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: So shall you do [כֵּן תַּעֲשֶׂה] as you have spoken [כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ].

**18:6** And Abraham hastened [וַיְמַהֵר אַבְרָהָם] into the tent [הָאֹהֱלָה] to Sarah [אֶל-שָׂרָה] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Make haste [מַהֲרִי] — three measures [שְׁלֹשׁ סְאִים] of fine flour [קֶמַח סֹלֶת] — knead it [לוּשִׁי] and make cakes [וַעֲשִׂי עֻגוֹת].

**18:7** And to the herd [וְאֶל-הַבָּקָר] Abraham ran [רָץ אַבְרָהָם] and he took [וַיִּקַּח] a calf [בֶּן-בָּקָר] tender and good [רַךְ וָטוֹב] and he gave it [וַיִּתֵּן] to the young man [אֶל-הַנַּעַר] and he hastened [וַיְמַהֵר] to prepare it [לַעֲשׂוֹת אֹתוֹ].

**18:8** And he took [וַיִּקַּח] butter [חֶמְאָה] and milk [וְחָלָב] and the calf [וּבֶן-הַבָּקָר] which he had prepared [אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה] and he placed [וַיִּתֵּן] before them [לִפְנֵיהֶם] and he was standing over them [וְהוּא-עֹמֵד עֲלֵיהֶם] under the tree [תַּחַת הָעֵץ] and they ate [וַיֹּאכֵלוּ].

---

*The Promise — Sarah Shall Bear a Son (vv. 9–15)*

**18:9** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] to him [אֵלָיו]: Where is Sarah your wife [אַיֵּה שָׂרָה אִשְׁתֶּךָ]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Look [הִנֵּה] — in the tent [בָּאֹהֶל].

**18:10** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Returning I will return [שׁוֹב אָשׁוּב] to you [אֵלֶיךָ] at the appointed time [כָּעֵת חַיָּה] and look [וְהִנֵּה-בֵן] — a son [לְשָׂרָה אִשְׁתֶּךָ] to Sarah your wife — and Sarah [וְשָׂרָה] was listening [שֹׁמַעַת] at the entrance of the tent [פֶּתַח הָאֹהֶל] and it was behind him [וְהוּא אַחֲרָיו].

**18:11** And Abraham and Sarah [וְאַבְרָהָם וְשָׂרָה] were old [זְקֵנִים] — coming into days [בָּאִים בַּיָּמִים] — it had ceased to be for Sarah [חָדַל לִהְיוֹת לְשָׂרָה] after the manner of women [אֹרַח כַּנָּשִׁים].

**18:12** And Sarah laughed [וַתִּצְחַק שָׂרָה] within herself [בְּקִרְבָּהּ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: After my being worn out [אַחֲרֵי בְלֹתִי] was there to me [הָיְתָה-לִּי] pleasure [עֶדְנָה] and my lord [וַאדֹנִי] has grown old [זָקֵן]?

**18:13** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה] to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם]: Why [לָמָּה] is this — Sarah laughed [זֶה צָחֲקָה שָׂרָה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Is it indeed true [הַאַף אֻמְנָם] that I will bear [אֵלֵד] — and I have grown old [וַאֲנִי זָקַנְתִּי]?

**18:14** Is anything too wonderful for the LORD [הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה דָּבָר]? At the appointed time [לַמּוֹעֵד] I will return to you [אָשׁוּב אֵלֶיךָ] — at the time of life [כָּעֵת חַיָּה] — and Sarah shall have a son [וּלְשָׂרָה בֵן].

**18:15** And Sarah denied [וַתְּכַחֵשׁ שָׂרָה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: I did not laugh [לֹא צָחַקְתִּי] — for she was afraid [כִּי יָרֵאָה] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: No — for you laughed [לֹא כִּי-צָחַקְתְּ].

---

*The Men Turn Toward Sodom — Divine Deliberation (vv. 16–21)*

**18:16** And the men rose up [וַיָּקֻמוּ מִשָּׁם הָאֲנָשִׁים] from there [מִשָּׁם] and they looked down [וַיַּשְׁקִיפוּ] upon the face of Sodom [עַל-פְּנֵי סְדֹם] and Abraham was walking [וְאַבְרָהָם הֹלֵךְ] with them [עִמָּם] to see them off [לְשַׁלְּחָם].

**18:17** And the LORD said [וַיהוָה אָמַר]: Am I hiding [הַמְכַסֶּה אֲנִי] from Abraham [מֵאַבְרָהָם] what I am doing [אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי עֹשֶׂה]?

**18:18** And Abraham will indeed become [וְאַבְרָהָם הָיוֹ יִהְיֶה] a great and mighty nation [לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל וְעָצוּם] and all the nations of the earth [וְנִבְרְכוּ בוֹ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ] will bless themselves in him.

**18:19** For I have known him [כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו] — that he will command [לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר יְצַוֶּה] his sons [אֶת-בָּנָיו] and his household after him [וְאֶת-בֵּיתוֹ אַחֲרָיו] and they will keep [וְשָׁמְרוּ] the way of the LORD [דֶּרֶךְ יְהוָה] to do righteousness and justice [לַעֲשׂוֹת צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט] — so that [לְמַעַן] the LORD may bring upon Abraham [הָבִיא יְהוָה עַל-אַבְרָהָם] what he has spoken concerning him [אֵת אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר עָלָיו].

**18:20** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה]: The outcry of Sodom and Gomorrah [זַעֲקַת סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה] — because it is great [כִּי-רָבָּה] — and their sin [וְחַטָּאתָם] because it is exceedingly heavy [כִּי כָבְדָה מְאֹד] —

**18:21** I will go down [אֵרְדָה-נָּא] and I will see [וְאֶרְאֶה] whether according to its outcry [הַכְּצַעֲקָתָהּ] that has come to me [הַבָּאָה אֵלַי] they have done completely [עָשׂוּ כָּלָה] — and if not [וְאִם-לֹא] I will know [אֵדָעָה].

---

*Abraham Intercedes for Sodom (vv. 22–33)*

**18:22** And the men turned [וַיִּפְנוּ מִשָּׁם הָאֲנָשִׁים] from there [מִשָּׁם] and they went [וַיֵּלְכוּ] toward Sodom [סְדֹמָה] and Abraham [וְאַבְרָהָם] was still standing [עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד] before the LORD [לִפְנֵי יְהוָה].

**18:23** And Abraham drew near [וַיִּגַּשׁ אַבְרָהָם] and he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Will you indeed sweep away [הַאַף תִּסְפֶּה] the righteous [צַדִּיק] with the wicked [עִם-רָשָׁע]?

**18:24** Perhaps there are [אוּלַי יֵשׁ] fifty righteous [חֲמִשִּׁים צַדִּיקִם] within the city [בְּתוֹךְ הָעִיר] — will you indeed sweep away [הַאַף תִּסְפֶּה] and not carry [וְלֹא-תִשָּׂא] for the place [לַמָּקוֹם] for the sake of fifty righteous [לְמַעַן חֲמִשִּׁים הַצַּדִּיקִם] who are within it [אֲשֶׁר בְּקִרְבָּהּ]?

**18:25** Far be it from you [חָלִילָה לְּךָ] to do [מֵעֲשֹׂת] according to this thing [כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה] — to put to death [לְהָמִית] the righteous with the wicked [צַדִּיק עִם-רָשָׁע] — that the righteous should be [וְהָיָה כַצַּדִּיק כָּרָשָׁע] as the wicked — far be it from you [חָלִילָה לָּךְ] — shall not the Judge of all the earth [הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל-הָאָרֶץ] do justice [לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט]?

**18:26** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה]: If I find [אִם-אֶמְצָא] in Sodom [בִּסְדֹם] fifty righteous [חֲמִשִּׁים צַדִּיקִם] within the city [בְּתוֹךְ הָעִיר] — then I will carry [וְנָשָׂאתִי] the whole place [לְכָל-הַמָּקוֹם] for their sake [בַּעֲבוּרָם].

**18:27** And Abraham answered [וַיַּעַן אַבְרָהָם] and he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Look now — I have undertaken [הִנֵּה-נָא הוֹאַלְתִּי] to speak [לְדַבֵּר] to the Lord [אֶל-אֲדֹנָי] and I am dust and ashes [וְאָנֹכִי עָפָר וָאֵפֶר].

**18:28** Perhaps the fifty righteous [אוּלַי יַחְסְרוּן חֲמִשִּׁים הַצַּדִּיקִם] will lack five [חֲמִשָּׁה] — will you destroy [הֲתַשְׁחִית] all the city [אֶת-כָּל-הָעִיר] for the five [בַּחֲמִשָּׁה]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not destroy [לֹא אַשְׁחִית] if I find there [אִם-אֶמְצָא שָׁם] forty-five [אַרְבָּעִים וַחֲמִשָּׁה].

**18:29** And he continued yet [וַיֹּסֶף עוֹד] to speak to him [לְדַבֵּר אֵלָיו] and he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Perhaps there will be found [אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן] forty [אַרְבָּעִים] there [שָׁם]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not do it [לֹא אֶעֱשֶׂה] for the sake of the forty [בַּעֲבוּר הָאַרְבָּעִים].

**18:30** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Please let not the Lord be angry [אַל-נָא יִחַר לַאדֹנָי] and I will speak [וַאֲדַבֵּרָה] — perhaps there will be found there thirty [אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם שְׁלֹשִׁים]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not do it [לֹא אֶעֱשֶׂה] if I find there thirty [אִם-אֶמְצָא שָׁם שְׁלֹשִׁים].

**18:31** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Look now — I have undertaken [הִנֵּה-נָא הוֹאַלְתִּי] to speak [לְדַבֵּר] to the Lord [אֶל-אֲדֹנָי] — perhaps there will be found there twenty [אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם עֶשְׂרִים]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not destroy it [לֹא אַשְׁחִית] for the sake of the twenty [בַּעֲבוּר הָעֶשְׂרִים].

**18:32** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Please let not the Lord be angry [אַל-נָא יִחַר לַאדֹנָי] and I will speak only this once [וַאֲדַבְּרָה אַךְ-הַפַּעַם] — perhaps there will be found there ten [אוּלַי יִמָּצְאוּן שָׁם עֲשָׂרָה]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not destroy it [לֹא אַשְׁחִית] for the sake of the ten [בַּעֲבוּר הָעֲשָׂרָה].

**18:33** And the LORD went [וַיֵּלֶךְ יְהוָה] when he had finished [כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלָּה] speaking to Abraham [לְדַבֵּר אֶל-אַבְרָהָם] and Abraham [וְאַבְרָהָם] returned [שָׁב] to his place [לִמְקֹמוֹ].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 18:1–2 — The Identity of the Three Men (Key Crux)

**וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה** (v. 1) — "And the LORD appeared to him" — followed immediately by (v. 2) **וְהִנֵּה שְׁלֹשָׁה אֲנָשִׁים** — "and look — three men."

The narrative opens with the blunt statement that the LORD appeared to Abraham — and then Abraham looks up and sees three men. This collision between the divine appearance and the human visitors creates one of the most discussed cruxes in the entire Pentateuch. The text gives no explicit identification of the three men at the moment of their arrival. The reader is left to hold the tension: the LORD has appeared — and three men are standing there.

**The identification question has four main positions:**

1. **The LORD is one of the three men (rabbinic consensus, widespread patristic reading):** On this reading, YHWH and two angels are the three visitors. The LORD speaks in the singular in vv. 10, 13-14, while the "men" are referred to in the plural elsewhere. When two of the three depart toward Sodom in v. 22, they are called **מַלְאָכִים** (mal'akhim, "angels/messengers") in 19:1 — which strongly suggests that the remaining figure who stays and dialogues with Abraham is YHWH himself. The singular/plural alternation throughout the chapter supports this: one figure speaks as YHWH, two figures are messengers.

2. **All three are angels/divine messengers:** On this reading, YHWH "appeared" in the sense that the divine visitation occurred — the medium of the appearance being the three angelic messengers. YHWH speaks through or in the context of their appearance without being personally present as one of the three. The messenger = sender equivalence of the ancient world (an ambassador speaks as the king) allows this reading.

3. **All three are YHWH (theophanic appearance):** The singular addressee in v. 3 (**אֲדֹנָי** — "my lord" in the singular) and the singular speaker in v. 10 have led some to see the entire triad as a single divine appearance in three forms — an ancient theophanic tradition. The singular "he said" (vv. 10, 13) does not require this but is consistent with it.

4. **The text is deliberately ambiguous — a theophanic narrative using "men" as its vehicle:** The narrator does not resolve the identity question because the story is not about angelology but about the divine initiative of grace, promise, and justice. "The LORD appeared ... in the form of three men" is the narrative's way of presenting a divine-human encounter that exceeds easy categorization.

**The structural evidence:** Verse 1 sets the frame — the LORD appears. Verse 2 presents three men. The singular address in v. 3 (**אֲדֹנָי**, one Lord) followed by the plural address in v. 4 (**וְרַחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיכֶם** — "wash your feet" — plural) reveals that Abraham is aware he is addressing multiple figures even while using a singular address of honor. This oscillation is the textual signature of the narrative's deliberate ambiguity.

The departure in v. 22 is decisive: **וַיִּפְנוּ מִשָּׁם הָאֲנָשִׁים** — "the men turned from there" (toward Sodom) — while **אַבְרָהָם עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה** — "Abraham was still standing before the LORD." Two go; one stays. The one who stays is called YHWH and conducts the entire intercession dialogue. The two who go are identified in 19:1 as **שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים** — "the two angels." This is the most explicit identification the text provides and strongly supports position 1: YHWH as one of the three, two angels as the others.

The EMET translates plainly: "three men" — without imposing interpretation — while noting in these textual notes the full complexity.

---

### On 18:3 — אֲדֹנָי: Singular/Plural Shift (Key Crux)

**וַיֹּאמַר אֲדֹנָי אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ אַל-נָא תַעֲבֹר מֵעַל עַבְדֶּךָ** — "And he said: My lord — if now I have found favor in your eyes — do not pass by from upon your servant."

The vocative **אֲדֹנָי** is grammatically singular — "my lord" not "my lords." Yet in v. 4, Abraham switches to the plural: **וְרַחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיכֶם** ("wash your feet" — 2mp plural) and **וְהִשָּׁעֲנוּ** ("recline" — 2mp plural). In v. 5 again plural: **תַּעֲבֹרוּ** ("you shall pass on") and **עֲבַרְתֶּם** ("you have passed by"). Then in v. 9, "they said" (3mp plural) — the plural visitors speak together.

**The significance of the singular address in v. 3:** The word **אֲדֹנָי** (Adonay) is a special form. When written with the long-vowel final **-āy** form (**אֲדֹנָי**) rather than the short plural of honor (**אֲדֹנִים**, "my lords"), it is in fact the same form used throughout the Hebrew Bible as a divine name substitute for YHWH — the Masoretes pointed YHWH with the vowels of **אֲדֹנָי** as a reading instruction: whenever you see YHWH in the text, read **Adonay** (Lord). The scribal-Masoretic tradition (and many rabbinic readers) understood Abraham's address in v. 3 to be directed specifically to YHWH — not to all three men — which would support the reading that Abraham recognized one of the three as divine and addressed him alone with the divine-name substitute.

However, **אֲדֹנָי** can also simply be the polite singular of address to a human superior — "my lord" in the ordinary honorific sense. The ambiguity is irreducible from the consonants alone; only the vowel tradition's association of **אֲדֹנָי** with the divine name pushes toward the theological reading.

The EMET renders **אֲדֹנָי** as "My lord" (lowercase "lord") here — without capitalizing — to preserve the ambiguity: it may be address to YHWH specifically or address to the leader of the three men generally. The reader is invited to hold the ambiguity that the text itself holds.

The shift from singular address (v. 3, one lord) to plural imperatives (v. 4, wash your feet) and back again reflects the real situation of speaking to one presiding figure on behalf of a group — a common social convention in the ancient Near East and not necessarily theologically significant in itself. Yet in the context of this narrative, every linguistic detail carries weight.

---

### On 18:10 — כָּעֵת חַיָּה: At the Time of Life (Key Crux — Connection to 17:21)

**שׁוֹב אָשׁוּב אֵלֶיךָ כָּעֵת חַיָּה וְהִנֵּה-בֵן לְשָׂרָה אִשְׁתֶּךָ** — "Returning I will return to you at the time of life and look — a son to Sarah your wife."

**שׁוֹב אָשׁוּב** (shov ashuv) — the infinitive absolute construction for intensive certainty: "Returning I will return" = "I will certainly return / I will surely return." The doubling of the verb is not idiomatic but emphatic — a solemn affirmation. The divine pledge is conveyed through grammatical intensification.

**כָּעֵת חַיָּה** (ka'et chayyah) — "at the time of life." This phrase appears in 18:10, 14 and in 2 Kings 4:16-17 (Elisha's promise to the Shunammite woman, whose son also dies and is raised — the typological echo is deliberate). The exact meaning is contested:

- **"At the living time / at the time of life" (most probable):** At the time when life [has occurred] — i.e., in approximately nine months, when the child is alive. The phrase refers to the time of birth, when new life has come into being.
- **"At this same time next year" (LXX: εἰς τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον εἰς ὥρας — "at this time next season"):** The LXX reads it as a temporal reference: "at this time, at the appointed hour [next year]." This connects to 17:21 where the same time-reference appears as **לַמּוֹעֵד הַזֶּה בַּשָּׁנָה הָאַחֶרֶת** ("at this appointed time in the next year").

Both chapters (17 and 18) are thus declaring the same promise: the announcement of 17:21 is now reconfirmed in 18:10 — within one year, at the appointed time, Isaac will be born. The narrative is structured as a double announcement: God to Abraham alone (ch. 17) and the divine visitors to Abraham (and overheard by Sarah) at Mamre (ch. 18). The second announcement is the one that provokes Sarah's laughter — she hears it for the first time.

---

### On 18:12 — וַתִּצְחַק שָׂרָה: Sarah's Laughter (Key Crux)

**וַתִּצְחַק שָׂרָה בְּקִרְבָּהּ לֵאמֹר אַחֲרֵי בְלֹתִי הָיְתָה-לִּי עֶדְנָה וַאדֹנִי זָקֵן** — "And Sarah laughed within herself saying: After my being worn out was there to me pleasure — and my lord has grown old?"

**וַתִּצְחַק** (wattitschaq) — "and she laughed." The same verb that names Isaac (**יִצְחָק**) — the same root as Abraham's laughter in 17:17. The birth of Isaac will thus be bracketed by the laughter of both parents: Abraham's astonished inner laughter (17:17) and Sarah's incredulous inner laughter here (18:12). The child of promise arrives as the child of laughter — **יִצְחָק** (Yitschaq) — the one at whom both his parents laughed before he was born.

**בְּקִרְבָּהּ** (beqirbah) — "within herself." Sarah's laughter is interior — she does not laugh aloud. Like Abraham's laughter in 17:17 (**בְּלִבּוֹ** — "in his heart"), Sarah's laughter is private, inward. Yet it is known — the LORD hears what is said within Sarah's heart (v. 13-14).

**אַחֲרֵי בְלֹתִי** (acharey beloti) — "after my being worn out." The verb **בָּלָה** (balah) means "to become worn out, to waste away, to be used up" — used of worn-out garments (Neh. 9:21: "their garments did not wear out"), aging bodies, and deteriorating things. Sarah is describing her own body as worn out — past fruitfulness. The word is stark: she is not merely old but exhausted, spent.

**עֶדְנָה** (ednah) — "pleasure, delight." This is a rare word — a hapax legomenon in the Hebrew Bible (occurring only here). Its root **עָדַן** (adan) means "to be soft, delicate, to have delight/pleasure." The cognate **עֵדֶן** (Eden) — the garden of Eden — shares this root: Eden is the place of delight. **עֶדְנָה** in context most naturally means "sexual pleasure / sensual delight / the bodily pleasure of reproduction" — the pleasure associated with conceiving a child. Sarah is saying: after I have become worn out and exhausted, am I to have pleasure [i.e., conceive]? The word's rarity makes it vivid: this is the exact right word for what she means but does not quite say directly.

**וַאדֹנִי זָקֵן** (wa'adoni zaqen) — "and my lord is old." Sarah adds the observation about Abraham. This detail is notable: when the LORD quotes Sarah back to Abraham (v. 13), he omits the phrase "and my lord has grown old" — God quotes her as saying only "and I have grown old." This is the famous rabbinic example (b. Yevamot 65b; b. Bava Metzia 87a) of a deviation from the literal truth made for the sake of peace (**שִׁנּוּי מִפְּנֵי הַשָּׁלוֹם** — shinnuy mipney hashalom): God does not report Sarah's words exactly to Abraham because quoting "my lord is old" to Abraham might cause discord between husband and wife. The rabbis derive from this a principle: one may alter the truth for the sake of peace and for the sake of harmony in a household.

**The tone of Sarah's laughter:** Is it disbelief? Irony? Cynicism? The text refuses to specify. Abraham's laughter (17:17) was inner and accompanied by prostration — astonishment mixed with reverence. Sarah's laughter is inner and accompanied by self-deprecating reflection on age and bodily exhaustion. Both laughs are private. Neither is scolded harshly. But only Sarah is confronted — because she denies it (v. 15). The difference is not the laughter itself but the denial. When caught, she lies; the divine voice insists: "No — for you laughed." The laughter stands. The child will be named for the laughter of them both.

---

### On 18:14 — הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה דָּבָר: Is Anything Too Wonderful for the LORD? (Key Crux)

**הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה דָּבָר** — "Is anything too wonderful for the LORD?"

The verb **יִפָּלֵא** is the Niphal imperfect 3ms of **פָּלָא** (pala') — "to be wonderful, extraordinary, surpassing, impossible." The root **פֶּלֶא** (pele') in the Hebrew Bible designates the category of the miraculous, the extraordinary-beyond-expectation, the act of God that surpasses what the world has a right to expect: the exodus miracles are **נִפְלָאוֹת** (nipla'ot, "wonders"); the angel says to Manoah "why do you ask my name, for it is **פֶּלִאי** (peli', 'wonderful'?)" (Judg. 13:18); Isaiah 9:5 names the coming king **פֶּלֶא** (Wonder-Counselor). The word designates what lies beyond the ordinary power of the world — the realm of divine action.

**הֲיִפָּלֵא מֵיְהוָה** — literally: "Will a thing be too wonderful — away from YHWH?" — i.e., "Is there anything beyond the capacity of YHWH? Is there a 'wonder' that exceeds what YHWH can do?" The rhetorical question demands "No" — there is nothing too wonderful for the LORD. YHWH is the source of wonder; nothing is beyond his reach.

**The crux of translation:** English "too hard" (KJV, many others) or "too difficult" flattens the rich overtone of **פָּלָא**. The word is not merely about difficulty but about the wonderful-miraculous-extraordinary. The question is not "Is anything too hard for God?" (as if God were a powerful worker who might encounter a task beyond his strength) but "Is there anything in the category of the impossible-wonderful that lies beyond YHWH?" The answer reshapes the nature of the promise: the birth of Isaac is not merely an unexpected event but a **פֶּלֶא** — a wonder, a miracle, a surpassing act of God.

The same question recurs in Jeremiah 32:27: **הֲמִמֶּנִּי יִפָּלֵא כָּל-דָּבָר** — "Is anything too wonderful for me?" — there in the context of divine sovereignty over history, redemption from exile, and the restoration of Jerusalem. The echo is not accidental: the God who opens the barren womb at Mamre is the same God who opens the gates of exile and restores his people.

**LXX rendering:** **μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ παρὰ τῷ θεῷ ῥῆμα** — "Is anything impossible with God?" — using **ἀδυνατέω** ("to be unable, to be impossible"). The LXX shifts from the Hebrew **פָּלָא** (wonderful/beyond-ordinary-capacity) to the Greek notion of impossibility/inability. Luke 1:37 echoes this LXX rendering in the angel Gabriel's words to Mary: **οὐκ ἀδυνατήσει παρὰ τῷ θεῷ πᾶν ῥῆμα** — "Nothing will be impossible with God" — announcing the miraculous birth of Jesus to another barren/impossible situation. The connection is deliberate: the annunciation at Mamre and the annunciation at Nazareth are typologically linked through this verse, through the LXX rendering.

---

### On 18:17–19 — The Divine Monologue: Abraham Chosen for Justice (Key Crux)

**הַמְכַסֶּה אֲנִי מֵאַבְרָהָם אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי עֹשֶׂה** (v. 17) — "Am I hiding from Abraham what I am doing?"

This is one of the most remarkable moments in the Hebrew Bible: the LORD deliberating with himself about whether to disclose his plans to Abraham. The rhetorical question is spoken by YHWH to himself (or perhaps to the angelic messengers) — not to Abraham. It is an inner divine soliloquy, overheard by the reader. The question implies the answer: No, I am not hiding it — I am about to tell him.

**The reason given (v. 19): כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו** — "For I have known him."

The verb **יָדַע** (yada', "to know") in this usage is not merely cognitive awareness but **intimate relational knowledge** — the verb used for a man knowing his wife (4:1: "And Adam knew his wife"), for God's election of a people ("You only have I known of all the families of the earth" — Amos 3:2), for the deep covenantal relationship of knowing-and-being-known. God chooses to disclose his plans to Abraham not because Abraham is merely informed but because God **knows** Abraham — the covenant relationship is one of mutual transparency.

**The purpose of the knowing:** God has known Abraham **לְמַעַן אֲשֶׁר יְצַוֶּה אֶת-בָּנָיו... לַעֲשׂוֹת צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט** — "so that he may command his sons... to do righteousness and justice." The purpose of divine election is the embodiment of **צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט** (tsedaqah umishpat — "righteousness and justice") in the community that descends from Abraham. This is the goal of the Abrahamic calling: not merely personal blessing but the formation of a people who enact divine justice in the world.

**צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט** (tsedaqah umishpat) — the paired terms are the foundations of covenant ethics in the Hebrew prophets: "Righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne" (Ps. 89:15); Isaiah, Amos, Micah, and Jeremiah all build their ethical demands around this pairing. The announcement at Mamre is thus not merely about the birth of Isaac but about the mission of the Abrahamic family: to enact in history the righteousness and justice of YHWH.

---

### On 18:20–21 — The Outcry of Sodom: Zaqat / The LORD Descends to See (Key Crux)

**זַעֲקַת סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה כִּי-רָבָּה** — "The outcry of Sodom and Gomorrah — because it is great."

**זַעֲקָה** (ze'aqah) — "outcry, cry of distress." The same root as the cry of the oppressed in Egypt (Exod. 3:7: **זַעֲקַת** — "the cry of my people"). The outcry of Sodom is not simply the report of Sodom's sins reaching heaven abstractly — it is the cry of those who suffer under the wickedness of Sodom. Ezekiel 16:49-50 specifies that Sodom's sin was pride, excess of food, prosperous ease, and failure to help the poor and needy — as well as the "abomination" committed. The **זַעֲקָה** is the cry of the victims of Sodom's injustice — the poor, the alien, the vulnerable — who cry out to God.

**אֵרְדָה-נָּא וְאֶרְאֶה** (v. 21) — "I will go down and I will see."

The particle **נָא** (na') is a particle of entreaty or deliberation — "please, now" — used when the speaker is carefully attending to the action being initiated. **אֵרְדָה** (erda) — "I will go down" — the same verb as 11:7 ("Let us go down" — Babel) and 11:5 ("the LORD came down to see" — Babel). The divine descent to examine human wickedness is a recurring narrative pattern: God does not condemn from a distance but examines, investigates, comes down. The "going down to see" motif emphasizes divine deliberateness and justice: God does not act on report alone but on examination.

**הַכְּצַעֲקָתָהּ הַבָּאָה אֵלַי עָשׂוּ כָּלָה** — "whether according to its outcry that has come to me they have done completely." The phrase **עָשׂוּ כָּלָה** — "they have done completely / done destruction" — is ambiguous: it could mean "they have acted with complete wickedness" (describing the totality of their sin) or "they have brought about complete destruction" (the effect of their actions). The divine investigation is whether the reality on the ground matches the magnitude of the cry.

**וְאִם-לֹא אֵדָעָה** — "and if not I will know." This is the most theologically loaded phrase in the passage: the divine "I will know" (אֵדָעָה) — using the same root as "I have known him" in v. 19 — applied now to the investigation of Sodom. God will know — examine, judge, determine. The symmetry is profound: the God who **knows** Abraham in intimate election is the same God who will **know** the truth about Sodom through righteous examination.

---

### On 18:22 — עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה: Tiqqun Sopherim (Key Crux)

**וְאַבְרָהָם עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה** — "and Abraham was still standing before the LORD."

This verse contains one of the most celebrated **tiqqune sopherim** (תִּקּוּן סוֹפְרִים — "corrections/emendations of the scribes") in the entire Hebrew Bible.

**The scribal tradition records:** The original text read **וַיהוָה עוֹדֶנּוּ עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי אַבְרָהָם** — "and the LORD was still standing before Abraham." The ancient rabbinic sources (Mekhilta de-Rabbi Ishmael, Numbers Rabbah, Midrash Tanchuma, the lists preserved in the Masorah) list this as one of 18 (or in some lists, more) places where the scribes altered the text out of theological scruple. The original reading had YHWH standing before Abraham — a posture of waiting, of attendance — as if YHWH were standing in Abraham's presence while Abraham deliberated whether to intercede. This was felt to be inappropriately undignified for God: God does not "stand before" a human being. The scribes therefore reversed the subject — making Abraham stand before the LORD rather than the LORD standing before Abraham.

**The significance of the original reading (if preserved):** The image of YHWH standing before Abraham — waiting, as it were, for Abraham to begin his great intercession — is one of the most astonishing images in the Hebrew Bible. It would present YHWH in a posture of patient waiting before the one he has chosen to be the father of righteousness and justice. The intercessor has the LORD's attention: the Almighty stands, attending. The tiqqun (correction) removes this image from the text out of reverence but the rabbinic tradition preserves the memory of it — and in doing so, preserves the original theological shock.

**לִפְנֵי יְהוָה** — "before the LORD" — in the Masoretic text, Abraham stands before YHWH: the posture of the one in prayer before the divine judge. The entire intercession (vv. 23-32) proceeds from this standing. Abraham does not sit or prostrate — he stands, the posture of one presenting a case before a court. The prayer of intercession is framed as forensic speech: Abraham argues before the Judge of all the earth.

The EMET translates the Masoretic text as it stands — "Abraham was still standing before the LORD" — and notes the tiqqun so the reader understands the textual tradition.

---

### On 18:23–32 — The Great Intercession: Structure and Theology (Key Crux)

The intercession of Abraham for Sodom (vv. 23-32) is the paradigm of prophetic intercession in the Hebrew Bible. Its structure is careful and cumulative:

**50 → 45 → 40 → 30 → 20 → 10**

Six exchanges, each lowering the number of righteous required to spare the city. The descent is not uniform: 50 to 45 (minus 5), 45 to 40 (minus 5), 40 to 30 (minus 10), 30 to 20 (minus 10), 20 to 10 (minus 10). The argument never reaches below ten. Why Abraham stops at ten is not explained in the text — possibly because ten is the minimum for a viable community (later rabbinic tradition sets the minyan at ten precisely on the basis of this passage: ten are required for communal prayer), possibly because Lot's household — the only righteous family in Sodom — numbers fewer than ten and Abraham knows this and cannot press further without condemning the procedure itself.

**The structure of each exchange:**
- Abraham's appeal: **אוּלַי** (ulai, "perhaps") — followed by the conditional number
- Divine response: confirmation that he will spare for that number
- Abraham's renewed appeal: **אַל-נָא יִחַר** ("please let not the LORD be angry") — apology before pressing further

The combination of **audacity and humility** is the rhetorical genius of the passage. Abraham is relentless — he will not stop — but at each stage he acknowledges his lowliness: "Look — I have undertaken to speak to the Lord — and I am dust and ashes" (v. 27). The theology of intercession presented here is that the righteous intercessor may press the divine Judge with the logic of divine justice itself — and may do so precisely because God has disclosed his intentions (v. 17-19) to the one he has chosen and known.

**הַאַף תִּסְפֶּה** (v. 23) — "Will you indeed sweep away?" The verb **סָפָה** (safah) means "to sweep away, to consume, to destroy by overwhelming" — used of floods, fire, total destruction. The question is not merely "Will you kill the righteous with the wicked?" but "Will you sweep everything away without distinction?" The image is of a catastrophic judgment that does not discriminate.

**חָלִילָה לְּךָ** (chalilah lekha, v. 25) — "Far be it from you!" This is one of the strongest protest-formulas in Biblical Hebrew — a cry of repudiation, of moral indignation: "This be far from you! This is beneath you! This must not be!" The formula appears also in Job 34:10 (Elihu: "Far be it from God to do wickedness"), in prophetic protests against the attribution of injustice to God (cf. Gen. 44:7, 17; 1 Sam. 12:23; 20:2). Abraham is not arguing from sentimentality but from the nature of God: for YHWH to sweep away the righteous with the wicked would be a contradiction of YHWH's own character.

---

### On 18:25 — הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל-הָאָרֶץ לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט: The Judge of All the Earth (Key Crux)

**הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל-הָאָרֶץ לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט** — "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?"

This is the theological apex of the chapter — one of the most piercing rhetorical questions in the entire Hebrew Bible. It is Abraham's ultimate argument in the intercession: not merely an appeal for mercy but a challenge grounded in the very nature of the divine Judge.

**הַשֹּׁפֵט כָּל-הָאָרֶץ** — "the Judge of all the earth." **שֹׁפֵט** (shofet) is both "judge" (one who renders legal decisions) and "ruler/governor" (in early Israelite usage, the shofetim/Judges are rulers). "The Judge of all the earth" is a title — a divine epithet — that asserts YHWH's universal jurisdiction. He is not merely the God of Abraham, nor the God of Canaan, nor a local deity — he is the Judge of all the earth, the one whose legal authority extends over all peoples, all cities, all nations. Sodom falls within his jurisdiction. Every act of judgment he performs is therefore a public statement about the nature of divine justice.

**לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה מִשְׁפָּט** — "will he not do justice?" **מִשְׁפָּט** (mishpat) is "justice, judgment, legal right" — the doing of what is right in the legal-judicial sense. The question is not "Will God be merciful?" but "Will God act according to his own declared standard of justice?" The argument cuts deeper than an appeal to divine compassion: it invokes divine consistency. A judge who condemns the innocent along with the guilty is not doing justice — he is doing injustice. For YHWH, the Judge of all the earth, to sweep away the righteous with the wicked would be a self-contradiction: the Judge of all the earth would be committing an injustice.

**The structure of the argument:** Abraham has built to this carefully:
- Verse 23: Will you sweep away righteous with wicked?
- Verse 24: What if there are fifty righteous — will you not spare the place for them?
- Verse 25: **Far be it from you to do this** — to put to death the righteous with the wicked — far be it from you — **Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?**

The argument is: (1) the act in question would kill innocent people; (2) this would be morally monstrous — "far be it from you"; (3) and it would be self-contradictory — the Judge of all the earth would be acting unjustly. The third point is the most powerful: it appeals not to divine sentiment but to divine self-consistency.

**The answer implicit in the question:** The rhetorical question assumes its own answer: Yes, the Judge of all the earth will do justice. God accepts the argument. The question is whether justice requires the destruction of the city — and what the role of the righteous within it is for the purposes of justice. The intercession proceeds from this logic: if even a remnant of righteous people remains, justice requires their rescue and may require the sparing of all for their sake.

**Theological legacy:** This verse becomes foundational for the theology of intercessory prayer in Judaism and Christianity: the intercessor does not flatter God or simply beg — the intercessor argues from God's own nature and promises. Moses does the same at Sinai (Exod. 32:11-13: "Why should the Egyptians say...?") and after the spies' report (Num. 14:13-19). Jeremiah, the Psalms, and Job all employ this mode of argument. The great intercessors hold God to his own word — not as a challenge to his sovereignty but as the deepest form of faith: they believe that God's justice is real and that it matters what actually happens to innocent people.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 18

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 18:1 | בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא | παρὰ τῇ δρυῒ τῇ Μαμβρη | LXX: "by the oak of Mamre" — δρῦς (drus) = oak. MT **אֵלוֹן** can be terebinth or oak; LXX consistently renders it as δρῦς. The tree identification is traditional and may be correct. |
| 18:2 | שְׁלֹשָׁה אֲנָשִׁים | τρεῖς ἄνδρες | LXX: "three men" — exact rendering. The LXX does not identify them as angels here either, preserving the MT's ambiguity. |
| 18:3 | אֲדֹנָי | κύριε | LXX: "Lord" — κύριος (kyrios). The LXX uses κύριος for both YHWH and for **אֲדֹנָי** — the ambiguity of the Hebrew (divine title vs. human honorific) is absorbed into the single Greek κύριος. |
| 18:6 | שְׁלֹשׁ סְאִים | τρία μέτρα | LXX: "three measures" — σεάτον (seaton) is the LXX's transliteration of the Hebrew **סְאָה** (se'ah, a dry measure). The Greek word for the unit appears in Matthew 13:33 (parable of leaven: three measures of flour) — the Mamre scene may be in the background. |
| 18:10 | כָּעֵת חַיָּה | εἰς τὸν καιρὸν τοῦτον εἰς ὥρας | LXX: "at this time, at the appointed season/hour" — **καιρός** for **מוֹעֵד**/**עֵת**, **ὥρα** for **חַיָּה** ("living/life"). The LXX parses **חַיָּה** as a temporal reference rather than "of life." |
| 18:12 | עֶדְנָה | τρυφή | LXX: "luxury, soft pleasure, indulgence" — τρυφή (tryphē). The LXX captures the sensual softness of **עֶדְנָה** well: τρυφή = the pleasure of the body, soft living. The Eden connection (**עֵדֶן**/**עֶדְנָה**) is not reproduced in Greek. |
| 18:12 | וַאדֹנִי זָקֵן | ὁ δὲ κύριός μου πρεσβύτερος | LXX: "and my lord is an elder/older man" — πρεσβύτερος (elder). The phrase is preserved in the LXX, which does not reflect the tiqqun tradition of omitting it from the divine quotation in v. 13. |
| 18:14 | הֲיִפָּלֵא | μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ | LXX: "is it impossible?" — ἀδυνατέω. The MT's rich **פָּלָא** (wonderful/miraculous) is rendered as the simpler concept of impossibility. Luke 1:37 echoes this LXX form. |
| 18:19 | כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו | ᾔδειν γὰρ αὐτόν | LXX: "for I knew him" — exact rendering, using pluperfect ᾔδειν. The intimate covenantal knowledge of **יָדַע** is captured in the LXX as straightforward knowledge; the covenantal overtone requires Hebrew context. |
| 18:20 | זַעֲקַת סְדֹם | ἡ κραυγὴ Σοδόμων | LXX: "the cry of Sodom" — κραυγή (krauge = shout, clamor, outcry). Captures the urgency and loudness of **זַעֲקָה**. |
| 18:21 | אֵרְדָה-נָּא | καταβὰς οὖν | LXX: "going down therefore" — καταβαίνω (to go down, descend). The deliberative **נָּא** is rendered by the inferential **οὖν** (therefore) — a different connective. |
| 18:22 | עֹמֵד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה | εἱστήκει ἔναντι κυρίου | LXX: "was standing before the Lord" — exact rendering of the MT as it stands (tiqqun form). The LXX also reflects the corrected text. |
| 18:25 | הֲשֹׁפֵט כָּל-הָאָרֶץ | ὁ κρίνων πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν | LXX: "the one judging all the earth" — ὁ κρίνων (the one judging, present participle). The MT construct **הַשֹּׁפֵט** (the judge) = the LXX's articular present participle. The rendering is exact and preserves the rhetorical force. |
| 18:27 | עָפָר וָאֵפֶר | γῆ καὶ σποδός | LXX: "earth and ash" — γῆ (earth) for **עָפָר** (dust) and σποδός (ash) for **אֵפֶר** (ashes). The MT's "dust and ashes" expresses human frailty; the LXX's rendering is slightly less vivid (earth instead of dust) but functionally equivalent. |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 18 fragments are extant from Qumran. The chapter falls in a gap in the preserved manuscript material. The Samaritan Pentateuch agrees closely with the Leningrad Codex for Genesis 18 with minor orthographic variants only.

**Targum Onkelos on 18:1:** Renders the opening phrase as a straightforward Aramaic equivalent, and notably handles the **אֲדֹנָי** of v. 3 as a divine address — "before the LORD" — in keeping with the rabbinic tradition that Abraham perceived the divine presence among the three visitors.

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan on 18:1-2:** Identifies the three men explicitly as angels: Michael (who announced Isaac's birth), Gabriel (who overturned Sodom), and Raphael (who healed Abraham after circumcision and rescued Lot). This later Targumic tradition imposes the angelological identification that the MT deliberately leaves open.

**On 18:22 — Tiqqun Sopherim — Ancient Rabbinic Attestation:** The tiqqun (scribal emendation) in 18:22 is listed in multiple early rabbinic documents: Mekhilta de-Rabbi Ishmael (Beshallah, Massekhet Amalek), Numbers Rabbah 3:2, and the Masoretic marginal notation itself. The Masoretes marked it as a **תִּקּוּן סוֹפְרִים** — a scribal correction — preserving the memory of what they understood to be the original reading: "the LORD was still standing before Abraham." The EMET translates the Masoretic text and preserves the rabbinic memory in these notes.

**Josephus (Antiquities I.11.1-3):** Josephus paraphrases the Mamre narrative, identifying the visitors as angels. He describes the hospitality scene and the announcement of Isaac's birth, and gives an account of Abraham's prayer for the Sodomites — presenting Abraham as a righteous advocate seeking to rescue the innocent. He does not preserve the tiqqun tradition.

**Philo (De Abrahamo 107-166):** Philo's treatment of the Mamre theophany is extensive. He allegorizes the three visitors as representations of God the Father (the central figure) and two powers — the creative power and the kingly/governing power (Logos and Sophia or their equivalents). The vision of three who are one and one who is three becomes for Philo a contemplative allegory of the divine nature perceived through the eye of the soul. The literal-grammatical reading and the allegorical reading coexist in Philo; the EMET notes his reading as a significant trajectory in the text's reception history without endorsing it as the primary meaning.

**On 18:14 and Luke 1:37 — The Annunciation Connection:** The thematic and verbal link between Genesis 18:14 (LXX: **μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ παρὰ τῷ θεῷ ῥῆμα** — "Is anything impossible with God?") and Luke 1:37 (Gabriel to Mary: **οὐκ ἀδυνατήσει παρὰ τῷ θεῷ πᾶν ῥῆμα** — "Nothing will be impossible with God") is one of the most explicit typological connections in the New Testament. The Mamre annunciation (a divine visitor announces an impossible birth to an aged barren woman) is read by Luke as the pattern for the Nazareth annunciation (a divine messenger announces an impossible birth to a young virgin). The same God who opened Sarah's womb opens Mary's womb. Luke's Greek virtually quotes the LXX of Genesis 18:14.

**On 18:19 and Amos 3:2 — Election and Knowledge:** The divine declaration **כִּי יְדַעְתִּיו** ("for I have known him," 18:19) is the verbal antecedent of YHWH's declaration to Israel in Amos 3:2: **רַק אֶתְכֶם יָדַעְתִּי מִכֹּל מִשְׁפְּחוֹת הָאֲדָמָה** — "Only you have I known from all the families of the earth." The election-knowledge given to Abraham becomes the election-knowledge claimed by Israel as his descendants. In both cases **יָדַע** is not mere information but covenantal intimacy — the knowing of a spouse, the knowing of a people chosen and accountable.
